topic: A student learns their thesis advisor is leaving the university
raw_output:
[story 1]

The fluorescent lights of the university library hummed with an unnerving intensity. Elias tapped his pen against the worn cover of his thesis manuscript, the rhythmic click a frantic counterpoint to the silence around him. Every time he glanced toward the faculty lounge, his gaze snagged on Professor Albright, who was currently engaged in a hushed, intense conversation with a colleague. A cold knot tightened in Elias’s stomach. He reread the last chapter, the words swimming before his eyes, searching for some hidden flaw, some sign that this entire endeavor was about to collapse. His fingers twitched, wanting to pull the pages apart, to reshape the argument before it became irreparable. He pushed his chair back, the scrape of metal against the linoleum sounding deafeningly loud. He needed coffee. He needed to move.

[story 2]

“He’s just going to leave,” Sarah murmured, the words tasting like ash in her mouth. She kept running a hand over the smooth, cool surface of her laptop, her knuckles white where she gripped the edge of the desk. Outside the window, the autumn rain hammered against the glass, a relentless drumbeat mirroring the quickening pulse in her neck. She stared at the email confirmation blinking on her screen—Albright’s departure date. Her breath hitched, shallow and quick. She stood up abruptly, pacing the small space between her desk and the bookshelf, her movements jerky and uncoordinated. Each step felt weighted, a deliberate effort to ground herself against the sudden, dizzying sense of instability washing over her.

[story 3]

I found myself tracing the grain of the wooden desk with my index finger, the texture grounding me in the immediate reality. The air in the office felt thick, pressurized, as if the very atmosphere was vibrating with unspoken news. I kept looking toward the door, anticipating the moment he would walk in, the way he always did—calm, composed, radiating an aura of absolute certainty. But he wasn't coming. Instead, I focused on the stack of annotated research papers, my eyes scanning the footnotes with desperate speed, trying to absorb every piece of data, every reassurance, as if memorizing the structure was the only way to keep the building from shaking apart. My jaw was tight, a hard line set beneath my skin.
